facts.fact: Anthropic prompt caching reduces cached token costs by 90%: cache reads cost $0.30/M tokens vs. $3.00/M for fresh processing
Cache write costs 1.25× standard input for 5-minute TTL, or 2.0× for 1-hour TTL; subsequent reads within TTL pay only 0.10× standard input

Context rot is an architectural property of transformer attention: even with perfect information retrieval, LLM performance degrades 13.9%–85% as input length increases within the model's claimed context window
Lost-in-the-middle effect causes 30%+ accuracy drops for information in the middle of long contexts (>2,000 tokens)
LLMs exhibit U-shaped attention bias where tokens at the beginning and end receive higher attention weights, regardless of relevance
Position bias is amplified with model depth: additional transformer layers cause earlier tokens to be used more frequently in reasoning, intensifying positional preference
PoisonedRAG attack: 5 malicious documents inserted into a corpus of millions cause targeted AI to return attacker's desired false answers 90% of the time for specific trigger questions
Chroma's 2025 context rot research tested 18 frontier models including GPT-4.1, Claude Opus 4.6, and Gemini 2.5; every model exhibits performance degradation at increasing input lengths
LLMLingua achieves up to 20× prompt compression with only 1.5% performance loss on reasoning tasks
Models claiming 200K-token context become unreliable around 130K tokens; accuracy drops noticeably at 60–70% of claimed window for 1M-token models
Batch API discount is 50% across OpenAI and Anthropic for non-real-time processing
Cloudflare's internal AI engineering stack processes 241.37 billion tokens monthly through AI Gateway with 47.95 million AI requests in the last 30 days from 3,683 active users
Stripe's fraud detection improved card-testing accuracy from 59% to 97% for their largest merchants using domain-specific LLM foundation models
Context engineering (management of non-prompt context like metadata, API tools, tokens) has superseded prompt engineering as the primary discipline in 2024-2025 production systems
Prompt optimization can achieve 79% token reduction while producing equivalent output quality
RAG pipeline optimization: teams passing 4–8 long documents can cut input tokens by 50%+ by limiting retrieval to 2–3 shorter chunks or aggressively truncating irrelevant sections
Model routing: 60–80% of coding agent requests are routine; routing these to cheaper models produces 70–85% cost reduction
Context compaction: compacting a 200K-token conversation to 80K tokens (60% reduction) saves tokens on every subsequent call; over a 200-call session, savings compound across all remaining calls
Extractive compression using rerankers performs best for multi-document RAG: achieves 2–10× compression while improving accuracy by filtering noise
Five models support 1M-token context window: GPT-5.4 (via Codex), Claude Opus 4.6, Qwen 3.6 Plus, Llama 4 Maverick, and Gemini 3.1 Pro
Gemini 2.5 offers the largest readily available context window at 2 million tokens with native multimodal support (text, audio, images, video)
Anthropic Claude API pricing in 2026: Haiku $1/$5, Sonnet $3/$15, Opus $5/$25 per million tokens; compared to GPT-4.1 at $5/$15
Subagents in Claude SDK run in isolated context windows; intermediate tool calls and results stay inside the subagent, only final message returns to parent
Multi-agent orchestration enables parallel subagent execution, reducing code review time from minutes to seconds by running style-checker, security-scanner, and test-coverage agents concurrently
Memory in autonomous agents integrates four types: procedural (how), semantic (what), episodic (what happened), and working memory (live reasoning context)
Temporal knowledge graph architectures organize agent memory into episodic (atomic facts with timestamps), semantic (enduring patterns), and community subgraphs
Agent memory blindness: paging wrong information wastes context tokens; archiving too aggressively creates situations where agents don't know critical facts exist in cold storage
RQ-RAG decomposes multi-hop queries into latent sub-questions, improving retrieval precision for complex information requests
TREC 2025 RAG Track employed reciprocal rank fusion (RRF) combining SPLADE-v3 sparse and Snowflake Arctic-Embed-L dense retrievers as hybrid approach
BABILong (NeurIPS 2024) is a needle-in-haystack benchmark testing language models' reasoning ability across facts distributed in extremely long documents
Needle in a Multimodal Haystack (MM-NIAH, NeurIPS 2024) shows all MLLMs exhibit poor performance on image needles and fail to recognize exact image counts in long documents
UltraGist uses randomly sampled compression ratios to handle dynamic context while maintaining near-lossless performance in long sequences
AutoCompressor uses Recurrent Memory Transformer architecture to iteratively compress arbitrary-length context with summary tokens concatenated before next segment
Sentence-based chunking enables multi-stage summarization for long documents; performance on long scientific documents improves significantly with chunking strategies

Causal masking in transformer attention inherently biases toward earlier positions; this bias amplifies with model depth as additional layers attend to contextualized representations of earlier tokens

analogies.concept: Prompt caching (ephemeral cache reads at 0.1× cost)
analogies.analogy: Like database query result caching: you pay full price once to compute and cache the result, then subsequent identical queries hit the cache for 90% discount. Cache expires after 5–60 minutes (like TTL in CDN).

Like how humans reading a dense 50-page document absorb the introduction and conclusion but skip middle chapters. The LLM's attention mechanisms literally weight the beginning and end more heavily, regardless of content importance.

analogies.concept: U-shaped attention bias in long context
analogies.analogy: Like a conversation where people remember who spoke first and last most clearly, but forget who spoke in the middle—even if the middle speaker made the most important point.

analogies.concept: Context poisoning (injecting malicious content into RAG)
analogies.analogy: Like poisoning a well: a small amount of contaminant (5 malicious documents in millions) corrupts the entire water supply (affects all future RAG queries) for anyone drawing from it.

analogies.concept: Model routing (cost optimization via model selection)
analogies.analogy: Like triaging hospital patients: 60–80% of incoming cases are routine and don't need the most expensive specialist; send routine cases to a less expensive clinician and save resources for complex cases.

analogies.concept: Episodic vs semantic memory in agents
analogies.analogy: Episodic = diary entries (what happened on March 5th); semantic = encyclopedia (what is a semantic memory? timeless facts). Agents need both: current session context (episodic) + learned patterns (semantic).
Like assigning different tasks to different team members: each team member gets a fresh briefing for their specific task, their work stays in their inbox, and only the summary goes to the manager.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Larger context windows mean the model can handle longer inputs without degradation
common_misconceptions.reality: Context rot is an architectural property of transformers: performance degrades 13.9%–85% as input length increases, even within the claimed window. A 1M-token model becomes unreliable around 600K–700K tokens.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Prompts should put the most important information in the middle where it's most relevant to the question
common_misconceptions.reality: LLMs exhibit U-shaped attention: beginning and end tokens receive higher attention weights regardless of relevance. Critical instructions belong in the system prompt (beginning) and repeated at the end.

common_misconceptions.misconception: RAG solves context limitations; just retrieve more documents
common_misconceptions.reality: Retrieving 4–8 long documents often wastes 50%+ of input tokens. Tightly limited retrieval (2–3 chunks) cuts cost in half with no accuracy loss. Quality of retrieval matters more than volume.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Prompt engineering (writing better instructions) is the key to production LLM quality
common_misconceptions.reality: Analysis of 1,200 production deployments shows context engineering (metadata, API tools, token management, versioning) has superseded prompt engineering as the primary discipline.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Always use the most capable (most expensive) model for every request
common_misconceptions.reality: Model routers classify request difficulty and pick the cheapest model that can handle it; 60–80% of requests are routine and route to cheaper models, producing 70–85% cost reduction.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Token-level truncation (cutting the end) is the best way to fit long documents
common_misconceptions.reality: Document truncation loses information. Extraction-based compression (reranking to remove noise) achieves 2–10× compression while improving accuracy.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Prompt caching requires redesigning your application architecture
common_misconceptions.reality: Caching is declarative: mark stable sections with `cache_control: {type: 'ephemeral'}`. The API handles it transparently; just pay 1.25–2.0× on first write, then 0.1× on subsequent reads.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Context poisoning is unlikely; my RAG system retrieves from trusted sources
common_misconceptions.reality: PoisonedRAG demonstrated that 5 malicious documents in a corpus of millions cause 90% success rate for targeted manipulation. Public websites, forums, and Wikipedia can be poisoned.

common_misconceptions.misconception: More memory in agents means better long-term reasoning
common_misconceptions.reality: Memory blindness is a real problem: agents with too much archived memory don't know critical facts exist in cold storage. Paging the wrong information wastes context tokens.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Subagents are a way to run the same task multiple times in parallel for redundancy
common_misconceptions.reality: Subagents are for task decomposition: each subagent gets a specialized prompt and different task. Running the same task in multiple subagents wastes cost.
